title: PODCAST | The Spillover: What Will the Fed Become Under Chair Kevin Warsh? | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Former Fed Vice Chair Roger Ferguson argues that under new Chair Kevin Warsh the Fed is leaning hawkish, with core inflation stuck above 3% and an unresolved Iran war keeping energy prices elevated, making a rate hike more likely than an ease. Warsh has launched five reform task forces and AI is currently an inflationary, not disinflationary, force via surging capex demand for capital.

insights.claim: With core inflation stuck above three percent for five years and not quickly returning to two percent, the Fed under Warsh is more likely to hike rates than ease, contrary to the easing bias currently discounted in markets.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Position for higher-for-longer or a surprise hike; market pricing of cuts may be wrong-footed, pressuring rate-sensitive assets and the long end.
insights.quote: I would definitely be in the camp of thinking that it was probably, you know, time to hike.
insights.locator: I've written and said a few places

insights.claim: Markets are now pricing the possibility of one Fed hike this year and possibly one next year, with some commentary suggesting hikes could come earlier rather than later given Warsh's hawkish tone.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Watch front-end rate futures and the dollar; a hawkish repricing supports USD and pressures duration.
insights.locator: I think the markets are now pricing

insights.claim: Warsh's first FOMC meeting delivered rates on hold, no easing bias, a shorter statement, no specific Warsh forecasts or his own dot on the dot plot, and the announcement of task forces.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Signals a regime shift toward less forward guidance and more optionality; reduces predictability of Fed communication for traders.
insights.locator: rates are going to be on hold

insights.claim: Warsh has framed the 2% inflation target as unanimous and unambiguous, committing the Fed to delivering on it, which Ferguson warns may be harder than imagined for structural reasons.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: A credible hard 2% commitment raises the bar for cuts and risks policy staying tight longer than the economy can absorb.
insights.quote: the commitment is unanimous and unambiguous

themes.theme: AI as inflationary force via capex

insights.claim: AI is currently acting as an inflationary force rather than disinflationary, because it is driving a surge in capital expenditures that calls for more savings and should push market interest rates somewhat higher to equilibrate increased demand for capital.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Higher neutral/market rates from AI capex demand argue for structurally higher yields; bullish for capital providers, bearish for long-duration bonds near-term.
insights.quote: what AI is driving mainly is an increase in capital expenditures, calling for, you know, more savings to go out. And that means to me market rate should be somewhat higher

insights.claim: A major technology provider recently announced it will likely raise prices on consumer goods because surging input demand is pushing up its costs, indicating AI is not yet delivering disinflation.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Confirms near-term AI-driven cost pressure; watch tech hardware/consumer pricing as an early inflation signal.
insights.locator: I saw one of the biggest technology providers

insights.claim: Even if the Iran war resolves, energy-driven inflation may persist because infrastructure destruction has already occurred and it is unclear whether the Strait of Hormuz will reopen freely or with tolls and tariffs affecting prices.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Energy inflation risk is sticky regardless of ceasefire; keep an inflation hedge / energy exposure until Hormuz transit terms are clear.

themes.theme: Fed structural reform via task forces

insights.claim: Warsh has created five task forces covering communication, the inflation mandate, productivity and jobs, data collection, and a balance sheet framework, reportedly including outside experts and targeting proposals by year end.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Telegraphs the specific dimensions of Fed reform to monitor; communication and balance-sheet framework changes most directly affect market structure.
insights.locator: my understanding with these task forces

insights.claim: Well-led, consensus-building task forces have historically moved the Fed forward, with Ferguson noting the modern era of post-meeting statements and forward guidance originated from task forces he chaired late in the Greenspan era, though progress will likely be uneven and slower than hoped.
insights.speaker: Roger Ferguson
insights.so_what: Expect real but gradual reform; don't price in fast structural change to Fed communication or balance-sheet policy.
insights.quote: This is the kind of thing where you want to, you know, measure twice, cut once.
insights.locator: First, thank you for the kind words

insights.claim: The Fed dot plot is likely to be revised or done away with because it consists of 19 confusing conditional statements rather than a true forecast, and Warsh declined to submit his own dot.
insights.speaker: Roger Ferguson
insights.so_what: Loss of the dot plot removes a key market guidepost; traders will rely more on the statement and Chair tone.

insights.claim: The Fed never established a clear framework for when and how quickly to use or unwind its balance sheet as a monetary or financial-stability tool, and Ferguson expects Warsh to develop one as a top task-force priority.
insights.speaker: Roger Ferguson
insights.so_what: A formal QE/QT framework would change the predictability of liquidity provision; watch for rules on balance-sheet runoff pace.
insights.quote: all that has never actually been thought through or worked out
insights.locator: less an issue about forward guidance

insights.claim: Market participants treat the Fed's inherently conditional forward guidance as a foregone conclusion, creating miscommunication risk where unmet expectations are blamed on the Fed having misled markets.
insights.speaker: Roger Ferguson
insights.so_what: Discount Fed guidance as conditional, not committed; positioning that assumes guidance is binding is vulnerable to violent repricing.
insights.locator: Isn't it funny? I would have put the the shoe on the other foot

insights.claim: The 2013 taper tantrum showed how a single Fed comment, despite repeated prior signaling, triggered an outsized reaction with global contagion and an emerging-markets sell-off, illustrating cross-border spillover risk from Fed communication.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Fed communication shifts under Warsh carry EM contagion risk; watch EM FX and yields around Fed messaging changes.
insights.locator: I think about 2013 with the taper tantrum

insights.claim: Warsh has signaled press conferences should occur only when there is news, implying potentially fewer scheduled pressers when rates are unchanged.
insights.speaker: Roger Ferguson
insights.so_what: Fewer pressers concentrate market-moving information into fewer events, raising per-event volatility.
insights.locator: There is a question of, you know, how often

themes.theme: Fed limits and the single-tool problem

insights.claim: The Fed's single national interest rate cannot target individual sectors like housing or specific prices, and its goal is controlling inflation rather than propping up housing or equity prices; for housing, the supply-demand of long-term Treasury yields matters more for mortgage rates than the fed funds rate.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Don't expect a fed put or fed-funds cuts to rescue housing; the long end of the Treasury curve is the lever to watch for mortgage rates.
insights.locator: the goal of the fed is not to support the housing market

themes.theme: AI labor reallocation: China vs US models

insights.claim: China's universities have killed or suspended over 12,000 undergraduate degree programs and introduced 10,000 new ones in four years (about 30% turnover) as a top-down attempt to align labor supply with future AI-era job demand.
insights.speaker: Rebecca Patterson
insights.so_what: Signals state-directed workforce reallocation ahead of AI disruption; contrast with messier US market-led adjustment when assessing AI productivity timelines.
insights.locator: China's universities have killed or suspended

insights.claim: Ferguson frames AI as a collision between two economic-management models, China's top-down population steering versus the messier US market-driven approach, with the outcome of which adapts better to AI uncertainty unknown.
insights.speaker: Roger Ferguson
insights.so_what: A geopolitical/structural lens for AI investing; the relative adaptability of the two systems shapes long-run productivity and growth divergence.
insights.quote: this world of AI is going to be a collision of two views of how one runs an economy
insights.locator: No, look, I agree with you
